did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning-based model named MOON-NET, which combines Squeeze-and-Excitation based DenseNet (SE-DNet) for feature extraction and a Deep Belief Network (DBN) for classification, to detect and classify melanoma skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel deep learning model (MOON-NET) for melanoma classification. It details a specific methodology (SE-DNet and DBN), describes the dataset used (HAM10000), reports quantitative results (99.36% accuracy), and compares performance against existing models. Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE ICCCNP), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: MOON-NET: Deep Learning based Melanoma Skin Lesion Classification using SE-DNet and DBN
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Compute, Control, Network & Photonics (ICCCNP)
brief_summary: This study proposes MOON-NET, a deep learning model for classifying melanoma and non-melanoma skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The methodology employs an adaptive Wienmed filter for noise reduction and lesion cropping, followed by feature extraction via a Squeeze-and-Excitation based DenseNet (SE-DNet). A Deep Belief Network (DBN) is then utilized to classify the extracted features into melanoma or non-melanoma categories. The model's performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F1 score. The proposed MOON-NET achieved an overall accuracy of 99.36%, outperforming existing models such as CNN, RegNet, ShuffleNet, RNN, DNN, Faster-RCNN, and ConvNeXtV2. These results demonstrate the efficacy of the hybrid architecture in enhancing diagnostic accuracy for melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as malignant; one of the two primary classes in the binary classification task using the HAM10000 dataset.

Classified as benign; one of the two primary classes in the binary classification task using the HAM10000 dataset. The paper notes that squamous cell skin cancer and basal cell skin cancer are categorized as NME.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification of melanoma skin lesions into two discrete diagnostic categories: Melanoma (ME) and Non-Melanoma (NME), using the HAM10000 dataset. The model outputs class labels rather than continuous values.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset containing 10,015 JPEG dermoscopic images collected over 20 years from Queensland, Australia and the Medical University of Vienna, Austria. Images and metadata were stored in Excel databases and PowerPoint presentations.

use.training: Used for training the MOON-NET model (SE-DNet for feature extraction and DBN for classification).
use.validation: Used for evaluating the model's performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, F1 score).
use.testing: Used for evaluating the model's performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, F1 score).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper relies on comparative performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, F1 score) without statistical significance tests.

HAM10000.split: Not explicitly defined (used for overall evaluation)
HAM10000.notes: The proposed MOON-NET model achieved an overall accuracy of 99.36%. It improved accuracy by 3.92%, 9.06%, 5.38%, 7.75%, and 2.03% over CNN, RegNet, ShuffleNet, RNN, and DNN respectively. Compared to DNN, Faster-RCNN, and ConvNeXtV2, the model improved overall accuracy by 6.26%, 4.15%, and 6.15% respectively. The DBN baseline reported identical metrics to the proposed MOON-NET in Table II, likely indicating the DBN is the classifier component of the proposed model or a direct comparison point with identical performance in this specific table context.

preprocessing: Adaptive Wienmed filter (AWF) for noise reduction
architecture_details.hardware: Intel Xeon F8 CPU (3.5 GHz), 64 GB RAM, NVIDIA GPU (11 GB VRAM)

training_details.training_schedule: Greedy layer-wise training for each RBM in the DBN, followed by systemic improvements to reduce reconstruction error
training_details.epochs: Not reported (graph shown in Fig. 5b indicates training over epochs)

classification_or_output_head.output_layer: DBN output layers corresponding to the number of categories (2)

preprocessing_techniques: Adaptive Wienmed filter (AWF) for noise reduction and image quality enhancement
Lesion cropping to remove unwanted background regions and focus on the lesion area
conclusions: The proposed MOON-NET model, which combines Squeeze-and-Excitation based DenseNet (SE-DNet) for feature extraction and a Deep Belief Network (DBN) for classification, achieved an overall accuracy of 99.36% in classifying Melanoma (ME) and Non-Melanoma (NME) skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.
MOON-NET outperformed existing models including CNN, RegNet, ShuffleNet, RNN, and DNN, with accuracy improvements of 3.92%, 9.06%, 5.38%, 7.75%, and 2.03% respectively.
Compared to DNN, Faster-RCNN, and ConvNeXtV2, the proposed model improved overall accuracy by 6.26%, 4.15%, and 6.15% respectively.

The SE-DNet component effectively enhanced feature extraction by emphasizing channel-wise information, allowing the model to focus on relevant lesion characteristics such as texture, color, and structural patterns.
The DBN component successfully classified skin lesions into ME and NME categories based on the hierarchical feature representations learned from the SE-DNet.

The study acknowledges a limitation in that the model does not incorporate additional modalities such as clinical data or histopathological information, which may limit its ability to capture contextual and diagnostic nuances required for highly accurate melanoma classification.

Future work will focus on integrating multi-modal data, such as patient clinical history, histopathological findings, and other imaging modalities, to enhance diagnostic accuracy, robustness, and clinical relevance.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which, while diverse, may not fully represent global skin tone variations or other demographic biases present in broader populations.

The model is limited to dermoscopic images and does not incorporate additional clinical modalities (e.g., patient history, histopathology), potentially limiting its ability to capture contextual diagnostic nuances.
The paper does not report specific training hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, batch size, optimizer) or the exact train/validation/test split ratios used for the HAM10000 dataset.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the reported performance improvements over baseline models.

The implementation was conducted in MATLAB (2020b) on specific hardware (Intel Xeon F8, 64 GB RAM, NVIDIA GPU), which may limit reproducibility for researchers using different software environments or hardware constraints.
The paper does not address potential data leakage risks or confirm that the HAM10000 dataset was split to prevent patient-level overlap between training and testing sets.
External validation on independent datasets beyond HAM10000 was not performed, raising concerns about the model's generalizability to unseen clinical data distributions.
